payload.text:
Here's a detailed, ready-to-use builder prompt for your Medical Prescription Reader Dashboard:

---

# 🏥 Medical Prescription OCR Dashboard — Builder Prompt

---

## Project Overview

Build a **single-page Medical Prescription Reader Dashboard** that accepts prescription inputs (image upload or URL), processes them using the **Google Gemini API**, extracts structured data, and displays the results both as formatted JSON and a clean UI card layout — all in one split-screen interface.

---

## Tech Stack

- **Frontend:** React (single `.jsx` file) with Tailwind CSS
- **AI Backend:** Google Gemini API (`gemini-1.5-flash` or `gemini-1.5-pro`) using `@google/generative-ai` SDK
- **File Handling:** Base64 encoding for image/PDF uploads
- **No backend server required** — Gemini API is called directly from the frontend

---

## Layout & UI Design

Build a **full-screen single-page app** split into two equal panels:

### Left Panel — Input Panel
- App title: `💊 Prescription Reader` with a subtitle `AI-Powered Medical Prescription Extractor`
- A **Gemini API Key input field** (password type) at the top so users can enter their own key
- A **tab switcher** with two tabs:
  - `📁 Upload File` — drag-and-drop zone that accepts `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.pdf` files. Show a preview thumbnail if an image is uploaded.
  - `🔗 Paste URL` — a text input field where the user pastes a direct image URL (http/https). Show a preview of the linked image below the field.
- A large **`Analyze Prescription` button** (full width, primary color) that triggers the Gemini API call
- A **clear/reset button** to reset all inputs and outputs
- Show a loading spinner with the text `"Reading prescription..."` while the API is processing

### Right Panel — Output Panel
- Header: `📋 Extracted Prescription Data`
- Show an **empty state** (icon + text: `"Upload or link a prescription to see results"`) when no analysis has been done
- Once processed, display **two sub-sections** with a toggle between them:
  - **`Structured View`** — a clean card-based UI layout (see below)
  - **`JSON View`** — a syntax-highlighted, formatted JSON block with a **Copy JSON** button

---

## Structured View UI Card Layout

Display extracted fields in organized card sections:

### 👤 Patient Information Card
| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Patient Name | Full name |
| Age | Numeric |
| Gender | Male/Female/Other |
| Date | Prescription date |

### 🏥 Doctor / Clinic Information Card
| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Doctor Name | With prefix (Dr.) |
| Specialization | e.g., Cardiologist |
| Clinic/Hospital | Name |
| Contact / License No. | If present |

### 💊 Medications Table
A styled table with columns:
- **Medicine Name**
- **Dosage** (e.g., 500mg)
- **Frequency** (e.g., Twice daily)
- **Duration** (e.g., 7 days)
- **Instructions** (e.g., Take after food)

Each row should be color-coded alternately for readability.

### 🧪 Diagnosis / Symptoms Card
- **Diagnosis:** (e.g., Type 2 Diabetes)
- **Symptoms noted:** (if mentioned)

### 📝 Additional Notes Card
- Special instructions, follow-up date, refill info, warnings

### ⚠️ Confidence & Warnings Section
- Show a **confidence badge** (`High` / `Medium` / `Low`) based on how clearly the prescription was read
- If any field was illegible or uncertain, show a yellow warning banner: `"Some fields may be inaccurate due to handwriting clarity"`

---

## JSON Output Schema

The Gemini API must return data strictly in this JSON format:

```json
{
  "patient_info": {
    "patient_name": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Patient's full name as written on prescription"
    },
    "prescription_date": {
      "type": "date",
      "description": "Date when prescription was issued. Format: yyyy-mm-dd"
    }
  },
  "doctor_info": {
    "doctor_name": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Doctor's full name"
    },
    "doctor_signature_present": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Whether doctor's signature is visible on prescription"
    },
    "doctor_stamp_present": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Whether doctor's stamp is visible on prescription"
    }
  },
  "medical_info": {
    "diagnosis_primary": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Primary diagnosis condition name"
    },
    "medicines": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of prescribed medicines with detailed information",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "medicine_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the prescribed medicine"
          },
          "medicine_dosage": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Dosage of the medicine (e.g., '500mg', '10ml')"
          }
        }
      }
    },
    "diagnostics_tests": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of prescribed diagnostics/labs/blood tests with detailed information",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "medicine_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the prescribed test"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "clinic_info": {
    "clinic_name": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Name of the clinic or hospital"
    }
  }
}

```

---

## Gemini API Integration Logic

### For Image Upload:
1. Read the uploaded file using `FileReader` and convert to **Base64**
2. Send to Gemini using `inlineData` with the correct `mimeType` (`image/jpeg`, `image/png`, `application/pdf`)

### For Image URL:
1. Fetch the image from the URL
2. Convert the response blob to Base64
3. Send to Gemini the same way as an upload

### Gemini Prompt to Send:
```
You are a medical prescription reader AI. Analyze the provided prescription image or PDF carefully.

Extract ALL visible information and return it ONLY as a valid JSON object matching this exact schema: [paste schema here].

Rules:
- If a field is not visible or not applicable, use null
- For medications, extract every single medicine listed
- For handwritten prescriptions, do your best to interpret the handwriting
- Set "confidence" to "high" if text is clear, "medium" if partially legible, "low" if mostly handwritten and unclear
- Add any unclear or potentially misread fields to the "warnings" array
- Do NOT include any text outside the JSON object in your response
```

---

## Error Handling

- If no API key is entered → show inline error: `"Please enter your Gemini API Key"`
- If no file or URL is provided → `"Please upload a file or paste an image URL"`
- If the URL image fails to load → `"Could not fetch image from URL. Please check the link."`
- If Gemini returns non-JSON → show a fallback error card: `"Unable to parse prescription. Please try a clearer image."`
- If API call fails → show the raw Gemini error message in a red alert box

---

## Styling & UX Details

- **Color scheme:** Dark navy background (`#0f172a`) with card panels in `#1e293b`, accent color teal/emerald
- **Font:** Clean sans-serif (Inter or system font)
- **Left panel** background slightly lighter than right panel to distinguish input vs output
- **Animations:** Fade-in on results, skeleton loader while processing
- **Responsive:** Stack panels vertically on mobile screens
- **Copy JSON button** copies the full JSON to clipboard and shows a temporary `"Copied! ✓"` confirmation

---

## File Structure (Single File)

Since this is a single `.jsx` artifact, organize code into these internal components:

- `App` — root layout, state management
- `InputPanel` — left side (tabs, upload, URL, button)
- `OutputPanel` — right side (toggle, structured view, JSON view)
- `MedicationTable` — the styled meds table
- `PatientCard`, `DoctorCard`, `DiagnosisCard`, `NotesCard` — individual section cards
- `JsonViewer` — syntax-highlighted JSON display
- `useGemini` — custom hook handling the Gemini API call logic

---

**Designing the Dashboard**

I'm currently focused on the foundational architecture for this "Medical Prescription OCR Dashboard". I've decided on a single-page React application using `index.tsx`, `index.html`, `App.tsx`, and a `metadata.json` file. My design centers around a two-panel split-screen layout, with the input on the left and the output on the right. I'm also planning a dark theme, using navy and slate colors for the UI.


**Integrating the Gemini API**

I'm now integrating the Gemini API, specifically `gemini-3-flash-preview` for its speed and suitability for OCR. My plan includes features like file uploads (image/PDF), URL input, structured and JSON output views, loading states, and robust error handling. The application will be structured with distinct components: `InputPanel`, `OutputPanel`, and `StructuredView`, with the API logic residing in `geminiService.ts` and data types defined in `types.ts`. I'll strictly adhere to the defined data schema.


**Addressing the API Key Conflict**

I've hit a snag regarding the API key. The instructions explicitly forbid a user input field for the key, contradicting the initial prompt's request. I've decided to prioritize the system instructions, as they are more important. I will focus on implementing the API integration using `GoogleGenAI` and retrieving the key from `process.env.API_KEY`, as the instructions state, and ignore the earlier prompt. I'm proceeding with the `ai.models.generateContent` call, setting `responseMimeType` to `application/json` to adhere to the requested schema.


**Prioritizing System Instructions**

I'm now implementing the API integration directly using `process.env.API_KEY`, as the system instructions mandate, effectively overriding the conflicting user prompt. I will handle PDF uploads via `FileReader` to Base64 and will incorporate a CORS warning for URL-based image fetching, anticipating potential issues. I am also working to refine the JSON schema.

entries.description: HTML entry point with Tailwind CSS and Inter font
TypeScript definitions for the prescription data and application state
Service logic for Gemini API integration
Left panel for file upload and URL inputs
Right panel for displaying structured and raw JSON data
Structured UI display of extracted prescription data

entries.description: Main application component with layout and state management

payload.text: which gemini model you are using here?

**Identifying the Gemini Model**

I'm currently focused on pinpointing the specific Gemini model used within the code. I've examined `services/geminiService.ts` and am now parsing the file to find where the Gemini model is instantiated and utilized.

In the application code provided, I am using **Gemini 3 Flash Preview** (`gemini-3-flash-preview`).

This model was selected because it offers high-speed performance for multimodal tasks (like OCR and image analysis) and follows the latest system guidelines for efficient, high-quality extraction from medical documents.

Hi Gemini,

I want to add a new feature. This is all working fine. I want to maintain a history of prescriptions that I have digitised. The UI is working fine. Add a history section where I can see previous digitised RXs options.

Thanks.

**Developing History Feature**

I am now focusing on the "History" feature for MediScan AI. My goal is to allow users to view past digitized prescriptions. I plan to implement a system that stores RX data, displays them in a list, and enables the user to re-display a selected prescription with a click. I am considering the optimal data structure for efficient retrieval and display.


**Refining History Implementation**

I'm now zeroing in on the history feature implementation. I've updated `types.ts` with a `HistoryItem` interface. Now, I'm working on `App.tsx` to manage the history list in state, initially pulling from `localStorage`. I plan to update the state with each successful analysis and build a way to select items from history for viewing. I'll focus on the data structure and UX flow. I plan to use a tabbed "History" section in `InputPanel.tsx`, listing past analyses, potentially including patient names and dates.


**Finalizing Local Storage Strategy**

I've decided to stick with `localStorage` for storing the JSON data, keeping things simple. My plan is now finalized: I'll create a `HistoryList` component, add a "History" tab to `InputPanel`, and manage the history state in `App.tsx`, updating `localStorage` after each successful analysis. I'll include a way to view a history item to redisplay previous prescriptions. I'm checking technical constraints; `localStorage` limits seem sufficient.

entries.description: Add HistoryItem interface and update InputMode to include history
